claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children I'm asking about. Their names are Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I'll need to gather some details about each of your children to assess eligibility.

Let's start with Alex. How old is Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 5 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. And does Alex live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me here in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you. Now let's move on to Blake. How old is Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Is Blake still in approved education or training (such as school, college, or an approved training program)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Can you tell me if Blake is working, and if so, how many hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't working at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Is Blake claiming any benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't claiming any benefits in their own right.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Now let me ask about Charlie. How old is Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie is 19 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Is Charlie in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie is studying A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, thank you. Does Charlie live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie lives with me here in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Just to confirm - do all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) live with you, and are you the person responsible for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. All three of them - Alex, Blake, and Charlie - live with me and I'm responsible for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. One final question - is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these three children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on your circumstances, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

**Alex (5 years old):** ✅ **Eligible**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Since Alex is under 16 and lives with you in the UK, you normally qualify for Child Benefit.

**Blake (17 years old):** ❌ **Not Eligible**
Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. For children aged 16 or over, you can continue to receive Child Benefit only if they are under 20 and stay in approved education or training. Since Blake is not currently in education, training, or working, they don't meet the requirements for Child Benefit.

**Charlie (19 years old):** ✅ **Eligible**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie. Even though Charlie is 19, you still qualify because Charlie is under 20 and studying A-levels, which counts as approved education.

**Summary:** You can claim Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie, but not for Blake. If Blake returns to education or training in the future, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for them again at that time.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 5 years old and lives with you in the UK. You are responsible for Alex. Since Alex is under 16, you normally qualify for Child Benefit. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 17 years old. For children aged 16 or over, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Blake is not in approved education or training, is not working, and is not claiming benefits in their own right. Since Blake is over 16 and not in approved education or training, you do not qualify for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 19 years old and studying A-levels, which counts as approved education. You normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Charlie is under 20, is in approved education (studying A-levels), and lives with you in the UK. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children: Alex (5), Blake (17), and Charlie (19). They all live with the claimant in the UK, and the claimant is responsible for all of them. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of the children. Alex qualifies as they are under 16. Charlie qualifies as they are under 20 and in approved education (A-levels). Blake does not qualify as they are over 16 but not in approved education or training, which is a requirement for children aged 16-19.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 5 years old and lives with you in the UK. You are responsible for Alex. Since Alex is under 16, you normally qualify for Child Benefit. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 17 years old. For children aged 16 or over, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Blake is not in approved education or training, is not working, and is not claiming benefits in their own right. Since Blake is over 16 and not in approved education or training, you do not qualify for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 19 years old and studying A-levels, which counts as approved education. You normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Charlie is under 20, is in approved education (studying A-levels), and lives with you in the UK. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children: Alex (5), Blake (17), and Charlie (19). They all live with the claimant in the UK, and the claimant is responsible for all of them. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of the children. Alex qualifies as they are under 16. Charlie qualifies as they are under 20 and in approved education (A-levels). Blake does not qualify as they are over 16 but not in approved education or training, which is a requirement for children aged 16-19.
